questions.question: A winery can produce 1,200 bottles of Riesling or 400 bottles of Cabernet. The winemaker decides to produce 50 more bottles of Cabernet. How many bottles of Riesling must be sacrificed?
options: 50 bottles of Riesling, matching the increase in Cabernet production directly
150 bottles of Riesling, reflecting the trade-off ratio of three Riesling per Cabernet
400 bottles of Riesling, equal to the total Cabernet production capacity
1,200 bottles of Riesling, representing the entire Riesling production line
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 Cabernet = $1200 \div 400 = 3$ Riesling. For 50 additional Cabernet: $50 \times 3 = 150$ Riesling sacrificed.

questions.question: In 8 hours, Country Alpha produces 40 tons of Coal or 20 tons of Iron. Country Beta produces 30 tons of Coal or 10 tons of Iron. Which country has the absolute advantage in both goods?
options: Country Beta, because its Coal and Iron production levels are closer together
Country Alpha, because it produces more of both Coal and Iron in the same time period
Country Beta, because its opportunity cost of Coal is lower than Alpha's cost
Neither country, because each has a different production ratio between the goods
questions.explanation: Alpha produces more Coal (40 > 30) and more Iron (20 > 10) in the same 8 hours. Higher total output in both goods means absolute advantage in both.

questions.question: Using the same data (Alpha: 40C, 20I; Beta: 30C, 10I), which country has the comparative advantage in Coal?
options: Country Alpha, because it produces more Coal in absolute terms than Beta does
Country Beta, because its opportunity cost of Coal is 0.33 Iron versus Alpha's 0.5 Iron
Country Alpha, because its Iron output per worker exceeds Beta's Iron output
Country Beta, because it can produce both Coal and Iron in roughly equal amounts
questions.explanation: Alpha's cost of 1 Coal = $20/40 = 0.5$ Iron. Beta's cost = $10/30 \approx 0.33$ Iron. Beta has the lower relative cost in Coal.

questions.question: A country's Production Possibility Frontier is drawn as a perfectly straight line. What does this indicate about the economy's resources?
options: Resources are specialized and cannot easily move between different industries
Resources are perfectly substitutable between the production of the two goods
The economy is operating at a point inside its production frontier boundary
Opportunity costs increase as the economy shifts production toward one good
questions.explanation: A linear PPF means constant opportunity costs. Resources are equally productive in both sectors, so the trade-off ratio never changes.

questions.question: Unit labor requirements are $a_{LX} = 12$ and $a_{LY} = 3$ in the Ricardian model. What is the autarky relative price $P_X/P_Y$?
options: 0.25, calculated as the inverse of the labor requirement ratio between the goods
4, equal to the ratio of the unit labor requirements $a_{LX}/a_{LY}$
12, matching the absolute labor hours required to produce one unit of X
36, the product of both unit labor requirements multiplied together
questions.explanation: Autarky relative price $P_X/P_Y = a_{LX}/a_{LY} = 12/3 = 4$. One unit of Good X costs 4 units of Good Y.

questions.question: Where does the World Relative Supply curve exhibit a vertical jump segment?
options: At the world price where both countries are indifferent between the two goods
At the relative quantity where a country has fully specialized in its advantage good
At the point where world relative demand intersects the world relative supply curve
At the autarky price ratio of the larger of the two trading partner countries
questions.explanation: The vertical jump occurs because further increases in relative supply require a higher price to induce the next most efficient producer to enter the market.

questions.question: World Relative Demand for Good X is very low. Where will the equilibrium relative price $P_X/P_Y$ most likely settle?
options: At the average of the two trading countries' domestic opportunity cost ratios
At the opportunity cost of the country that has the comparative advantage in Good X
Above both countries' opportunity costs to ensure producers earn adequate returns
At exactly the midpoint between the two countries' internal price ratios
questions.explanation: With low demand, the equilibrium price settles at the first step of the RS curve, which is the internal cost of the most efficient producer of Good X.

questions.question: Home productivity is 5 times Foreign's in every sector, but Home wages are 6 times higher. What is the predicted trade outcome?
options: Home will export all goods because its absolute productivity is much higher
Home will import all tradable goods because its wage premium exceeds its productivity lead
Trade will be balanced with each country exporting exactly half of all goods
No trade will occur because comparative advantage requires equal wage rates
questions.explanation: Home's wage disadvantage ($6\times$) exceeds its productivity advantage ($5\times$), making Home's unit labor costs higher in every sector. Home imports all tradable goods.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, labor productivity increases in the country's export sector. What happens to the real wage measured in terms of all goods?
options: The real wage increases only in terms of the export good but not in terms of imports
The real wage increases in terms of all goods as higher productivity raises overall purchasing power
The real wage remains constant because technology does not affect the wage rate
The real wage falls because higher productivity reduces the number of workers needed
questions.explanation: Higher productivity in the specialized export sector increases the nominal wage relative to all prices, raising real purchasing power across the entire consumption basket.

questions.question: What defines the general equilibrium condition in the Ricardian model of international trade?
options: The point where each country's domestic opportunity cost equals its trading partner's cost
The relative price where world relative supply of the two goods equals world relative demand
The wage ratio at which labor is equally divided between the two trading countries
The exchange rate that balances the Current Account between the two trading partners
questions.explanation: General equilibrium requires that the global quantity of Good X relative to Good Y supplied matches the relative quantity demanded at the equilibrium relative price.

questions.question: A Ricardian country has 500 total labor units and $a_{LX} = 5$. What is the X-intercept of its Production Possibility Frontier?
options: 2,500 units, calculated by multiplying total labor by the unit labor requirement
100 units, equal to the total labor endowment divided by the unit labor requirement
500 units, matching the total available labor force in the country
5 units, reflecting the inverse of the unit labor requirement times labor
questions.explanation: Maximum X = $L / a_{LX} = 500 \div 5 = 100$ units. This is the X-axis intercept of the PPF.

questions.question: A trade policy benefits 1,000 consumers by \$10 each but costs one domestic producer \$20,000. How is this outcome classified in welfare economics?
options: A potential Pareto Improvement because the number of winners far exceeds the losers
Neither a Pareto Improvement nor a Potential Pareto Improvement, because total losses exceed total gains
An actual Pareto Improvement because the total number of beneficiaries is very large
A Kaldor-Hicks improvement because consumers could theoretically compensate the producer
questions.explanation: Total gains = $1,000 \times \$10 = \$10,000$, which is less than the \$20,000 loss. Winners cannot compensate the loser and remain better off, so it fails even the Kaldor-Hicks criterion.

questions.question: In the Edgeworth Box diagram, what does the Contract Curve represent?
options: The set of points where one consumer holds all of both goods in the economy
All possible allocations that are Pareto Efficient between the two consumers
The initial endowment point before any voluntary trades have taken place
The budget constraint facing each consumer at the equilibrium world price ratio
questions.explanation: Any point on the Contract Curve is an allocation where the marginal rates of substitution for both consumers are equalized, exhausting all mutual gains from trade.

questions.question: $P_A = 50$, $P_B = 80$, $a_{LA} = 2$, and $a_{LB} = 4$ in the Ricardian model. Which sector will produce output?
options: Only Sector B, because the price per unit of Good B is higher than Good A
Only Sector A, because the hourly wage of 25 exceeds Sector B's wage of 20
Both sectors equally, dividing the labor force between the two goods
Neither sector, because wages are equal and there is no incentive to produce
questions.explanation: Wage in A = $50/2 = 25$. Wage in B = $80/4 = 20$. All labor moves to the higher-paying Sector A until Sector B production ceases entirely.

questions.question: What feature of the Ricardian model allows it to predict complete specialization rather than partial specialization?
options: Increasing returns to scale that reward larger production volumes
Constant returns to scale with constant opportunity costs along the entire PPF
Diminishing marginal returns that eventually limit further labor reallocation
Government policies that mandate full specialization in the export sector
questions.explanation: Without diminishing returns, there is no rising marginal cost to deter shifting all labor into the more profitable sector. Complete specialization is the equilibrium outcome.

questions.question: In a many-good Ricardian model, how are goods ordered to determine which ones a country will export?
options: Alphabetically, with goods at the beginning of the list being exported first
By the ratio of Home productivity to Foreign productivity, from highest ratio to lowest
By consumer preference rankings, with the most popular goods exported first
By the absolute labor hours required, with the least labor-intensive goods exported first
questions.explanation: This chain of comparative advantage ranks goods by relative productivity ($A_{Home}/A_{Foreign}$). The relative wage determines the cutoff between exported and imported goods.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the import-competing good's price falls. Why does the capital owner in that sector suffer more than in the Ricardian model?
options: Capital depreciates faster when import prices fall in the Ricardian model
Immobile capital cannot be sold or relocated to a more profitable sector elsewhere
The Ricardian model assumes that capital does not exist as a factor of production
Capital owners always benefit from falling prices regardless of factor mobility
questions.explanation: Immobility traps capital in the declining sector. The owner cannot escape by selling equipment or shifting investment to an expanding industry, unlike in long-run models.

questions.question: The immobile factor model is particularly useful for explaining which real-world phenomenon?
options: Long-run technological convergence between developed and developing nations
The short-run political opposition to free trade from import-competing industries
Why all factors of production eventually benefit equally from international trade
The gradual equalization of wages across different sectors of the domestic economy
questions.explanation: The model highlights the immediate, concentrated economic pain suffered by specific groups before factors have had time to retrain, relocate, or be repurposed.

questions.question: Labor is perfectly immobile and the demand for the good it produces falls. What happens to the physical marginal product of labor in that sector?
options: The marginal product increases because fewer goods need to be produced
The marginal product remains unchanged because the quantity of labor is fixed
The marginal product decreases as workers become demoralized by falling demand
The marginal product falls in proportion to the decrease in demand for the good
questions.explanation: Physical MPL depends on the amount of labor employed. If labor cannot leave, the quantity is fixed. Only the value of that marginal product ($P \times MPL$) falls with the price.

questions.question: The price of the labor-intensive good falls by 5%. According to the Magnification Effect in the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, what happens to real wages?
options: Real wages fall by exactly 5%, matching the goods price decrease proportionally
Real wages fall by more than 5%, amplifying the change in the goods price
Real wages remain unchanged because they are determined solely by technology
Real wages fall by less than 5%, cushioned by capital market adjustments
questions.explanation: The Magnification Effect states that factor prices change more than proportionally to goods price changes. A 5% price decline causes a larger than 5% decline in the intensive factor's return.

questions.question: Country 1 has 1,000 machines and 2,000 workers. Country 2 has 200 machines and 500 workers. Which country is relatively labor-abundant?
options: Country 1, because it has a larger absolute number of workers in total
Country 2, because its labor-to-capital ratio of 2.5 exceeds Country 1's ratio of 2.0
Country 1, because it has more machines in absolute terms than Country 2
Neither country, because both have more workers than machines in absolute terms
questions.explanation: Country 1 L/K = $2000/1000 = 2.0$. Country 2 L/K = $500/200 = 2.5$. Country 2 has more labor per unit of capital and is relatively labor-abundant.

questions.question: In the H-O model, international trade leads to factor price convergence between countries. Why does this occur?
options: Workers migrate from low-wage countries to high-wage countries seeking better pay
Exporting a good is equivalent to indirectly exporting the services of the abundant factor
Central banks coordinate monetary policy to equalize interest rates and wages globally
The World Trade Organization mandates uniform wages across all member countries
questions.explanation: By exporting labor-intensive goods, a country effectively sells the services of its abundant labor. This increases domestic demand for labor and bids up wages toward global levels.

questions.question: What is the core statement of the Heckscher-Ohlin theorem regarding international trade patterns?
options: Countries trade based on absolute productivity differences across all industries
A country will export the good that uses its relatively abundant factor most intensively
Trade patterns are determined entirely by consumer preferences and demand conditions
Only countries with different technologies can engage in mutually beneficial trade
questions.explanation: The H-O theorem predicts that a country's comparative advantage lies in goods that intensively use the factors of production the country possesses in relative abundance.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, $P_X$ increases by 10% while $P_Y$ remains constant. By approximately how much will the nominal wage increase?
options: By exactly 10%, matching the full increase in the price of Good X
By an amount between zero and 10%, dampened by diminishing returns to labor
By more than 10%, amplified by the Magnification Effect on factor prices
By zero percent, because wages are anchored by the constant price of Good Y
questions.explanation: Labor moves to X, increasing supply there and lowering $MPL_X$. This dampens the nominal wage increase below the full percentage price rise.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, the price of Good Y increases. What happens to the return of the specific factor in Sector X?
options: The return in X increases because the price rise boosts the entire economy
The return in X decreases as labor is drawn away and wages in X rise
The return in X remains constant because specific factors are fixed in place
The return in X initially rises but falls as labor reallocates between sectors
questions.explanation: Higher $P_Y$ draws mobile labor out of Sector X, raising the wage in X. The higher wage reduces the residual surplus available for the specific factor owners in Sector X.

questions.question: An energy shock destroys half of a country's industrial capital, which is a specific factor. What happens to the wages of mobile labor in the Specific Factor Model?
options: Wages rise because capital scarcity makes the remaining capital more valuable
Wages fall because less capital reduces the marginal productivity of labor in industry
Wages remain constant because they are set in global competitive labor markets
Wages rise in agriculture but fall in manufacturing as workers relocate
questions.explanation: Less industrial capital means each worker in that sector is less productive. The labor demand curve shifts inward, lowering the equilibrium nominal wage.

questions.question: What determines the slope of the Production Possibility Frontier in the Specific Factor Model?
options: The ratio of the specific factor endowments in each of the two sectors
The negative ratio of the marginal products of labor in the two sectors
The world relative price ratio established through international trade
The marginal rate of substitution between the two goods for all consumers
questions.explanation: PPF slope = $-MPL_Y / MPL_X$, reflecting the opportunity cost of reallocating one unit of mobile labor between the two sectors at the margin.

questions.question: In the Krugman monopolistic competition model, trade opens between two countries. What happens to the total number of varieties available to consumers in each country?
options: The number of varieties decreases because trade eliminates smaller domestic firms
The number of varieties increases as consumers access products from both countries' markets
The number of varieties remains the same because each country keeps its own firms
Varieties increase in the larger country but decrease in the smaller trading partner
questions.explanation: While some domestic varieties may disappear, consumers now have access to the combined set of varieties produced in both the Home and Foreign markets.

questions.question: What concept explains how a small country or region can become and remain the dominant global producer in an industry despite having no inherent natural advantage?
options: Comparative advantage based on relative factor abundance and technology
Historical accident combined with external economies of scale and first-mover benefits
Government industrial policy that permanently subsidizes domestic producers
This outcome is impossible because trade always follows natural resource endowments
questions.explanation: Once an industry establishes itself early in a location (like Silicon Valley), accumulated external economies create barriers that make it very difficult for latecomers to compete.

questions.question: What cost structure typically gives rise to a natural monopoly in international trade?
options: Low fixed costs combined with very high and rising marginal costs of production
Very high fixed costs combined with low and constant marginal costs of production
Equal fixed and variable costs that remain constant at all levels of total output
Zero fixed costs with marginal costs that increase linearly with production volume
questions.explanation: When fixed costs are very high and marginal costs are low, one firm can supply the entire market at a lower average cost than two or more firms could achieve.

questions.question: What is trade diversion in the context of a Free Trade Agreement or regional trade bloc?
options: Creating entirely new trade flows between members that did not previously exist
Replacing low-cost imports from non-members with higher-cost imports from bloc members
Redirecting domestic production toward goods that were previously imported from abroad
Relocating factories from one member country to another to take advantage of lower wages
questions.explanation: Trade diversion is welfare-reducing. The internal tariff removal makes a member's goods artificially cheaper than a more efficient non-member's products.

questions.question: What is the net national welfare effect of a 10% import quota imposed by a small country?
options: Positive, because the quota protects domestic jobs and industrial capacity
Negative, due to deadweight losses from production and consumption distortions
Zero, because the quota's costs and benefits exactly cancel each other out
Uncertain, depending on whether the quota licenses are auctioned by the government
questions.explanation: A small country cannot improve its terms of trade. The quota creates deadweight losses without any offsetting benefit, reducing national welfare.

questions.question: In what type of market structure can a strategic export subsidy successfully shift profits from a foreign firm to a domestic firm?
options: Perfect competition with many small firms and homogeneous product offerings
An oligopoly where firms' output decisions strategically affect their rivals' profits
A pure monopoly where only one firm exists in the entire global market
Strategic subsidies are equally effective in all types of market structures
questions.explanation: In an oligopoly (like Airbus vs. Boeing), a subsidy can credibly commit the domestic firm to higher output, deterring the foreign rival and shifting profits home.

questions.question: What is the rent-seeking cost associated with an import quota system?
options: Higher consumer prices caused by the reduced supply of imported goods in the market
Real resources wasted by firms competing to obtain valuable import licenses from the government
The government revenue lost because quotas do not automatically generate tariff income
Reduced product variety as foreign firms exit the restricted domestic market entirely
questions.explanation: Firms spend money on lobbying, legal fees, and political influence to capture quota rents. These resources are wasted from society's perspective because they produce no output.

questions.question: A large country imposes a tariff, and foreign exporters respond by reducing their pre-tariff prices to maintain market share. What is this phenomenon called?
options: Complete pass-through, where consumers bear the entire cost of the tariff
Partial pass-through, where foreign firms absorb part of the tariff through lower prices
Tariff absorption, where the domestic government compensates foreign exporters
Reciprocal dumping, where both countries lower prices to capture market share
questions.explanation: Foreign exporters accept lower pre-tariff prices to remain competitive. The domestic price rises by less than the full tariff, generating a terms-of-trade improvement for the importing country.

questions.question: How is the deadweight loss of a tariff represented graphically on a standard partial equilibrium diagram?
options: As a single rectangle representing the total government revenue from the tariff
As two separate triangles: one for production distortion and one for consumption distortion
As a trapezoid capturing the transfer from consumers to domestic producers
As the area between the domestic demand curve and the world price plus tariff line
questions.explanation: One triangle captures the excess cost of inefficient domestic production. The other triangle captures the lost consumer surplus from reduced consumption.

questions.question: A small country applies a 20% tariff to a good with a world price of \$100. How is the change in consumer surplus best described?
options: Consumer surplus increases because domestic production expands and creates jobs
A decrease equal to the price increase times the average quantity consumed, plus lost surplus from reduced purchases
Consumer surplus remains unchanged because the tariff only affects producer welfare
A transfer from consumers to producers with no net change in total consumer surplus
questions.explanation: Consumers pay \$120 instead of \$100 on units they still buy, and they lose the surplus from units they can no longer afford at the higher protected price.

questions.question: What is the optimal tariff rate for a small country that cannot influence world prices through its own trade policy?
options: The rate that maximizes government revenue collected from all imported goods
Zero, because any positive tariff creates deadweight loss with no terms-of-trade gain
The rate that equalizes the domestic price with the autarky equilibrium price level
The rate recommended by the World Trade Organization for developing economies
questions.explanation: A small country faces fixed world prices. Any positive tariff only distorts production and consumption without generating any offsetting terms-of-trade benefit.

questions.question: How does a global quota differ from a selective quota in international trade policy?
options: Global quotas apply to all goods while selective quotas target only one product
A global quota sets a total import limit regardless of origin, while a selective quota targets specific countries
Global quotas are set by the WTO while selective quotas are unilateral measures
There is no difference because both types of quotas restrict imports equally
questions.explanation: A global quota caps total imports from all sources combined. A selective quota restricts imports only from named countries, potentially violating MFN principles.

questions.question: Which trade policy instrument is commonly used as a safeguard mechanism to protect domestic industries from sudden import surges?
options: A voluntary export restraint negotiated with the principal foreign supplying country
A tariff-rate quota, which applies a low tariff up to a threshold and a high tariff beyond it
An outright embargo that completely bans all imports of the sensitive product
A permanent ad valorem tariff set at the maximum bound rate under WTO commitments
questions.explanation: A TRQ allows limited imports at a low tariff rate. Once imports exceed the threshold, a much higher tariff applies, cushioning domestic producers from import surges.

questions.question: What is the national defense argument used to justify protecting a domestic steel industry?
options: Steel tariffs generate government revenue needed to fund the military budget
Self-sufficiency in strategic materials is necessary for security, regardless of economic efficiency cost
A strong steel industry creates jobs for military veterans returning from service
Foreign steel is always of inferior quality and unsuitable for military applications
questions.explanation: The national defense argument is a non-economic justification. It holds that reliance on foreign sources for critical wartime materials poses an unacceptable security risk.

questions.question: A Voluntary Export Restraint limits the quantity of goods a foreign country can export. How do foreign firms typically respond?
options: They reduce quality to cut costs and maintain profitability at lower sales volumes
They upgrade the quality of their exports to maximize profit per unit under the quantity cap
They exit the market entirely because VERs make exporting completely unprofitable
They shift production to the importing country to bypass the export restriction
questions.explanation: Since the number of units is capped, exporters shift their product mix toward higher-priced, higher-margin models to maximize total revenue under the quantity constraint.

questions.question: A country's Private Savings is \$400 billion, its Government Budget Deficit is \$100 billion, and Investment is \$250 billion. What is the Current Account balance?
options: +\$550 billion, reflecting the sum of private savings and the government deficit
+\$50 billion, calculated as private savings minus investment minus the budget deficit
–\$50 billion, indicating the country is a net borrower from the rest of the world
–\$250 billion, equal to the total investment level in the domestic economy
questions.explanation: $CA = S_{private} - I - (G - T) = \$400 - \$250 - \$100 = +\$50$ billion. The country runs a Current Account surplus.

questions.question: What does the Twin Deficits hypothesis propose about the relationship between fiscal and external balances?
options: Trade deficits and budget deficits always move in opposite directions over time
A large government budget deficit tends to be associated with a large current account deficit
Only countries with budget surpluses can sustainably run current account deficits
The two deficits are completely unrelated because one is fiscal and the other is external
questions.explanation: If private saving and investment are relatively stable, an increase in the government budget deficit reduces national saving and must be financed by foreign capital, worsening the Current Account.

questions.question: The United States provides a unilateral transfer payment as foreign aid to a developing nation. How is this recorded in the US Balance of Payments?
options: As a credit in the Current Account because the US is providing assistance
As a debit in the Current Account under the Secondary Income category
As a credit in the Financial Account reflecting the outflow of government funds
As a debit in the Financial Account because official reserves are being depleted
questions.explanation: Unilateral transfers are outflows with nothing received in return. Foreign aid is recorded as a debit in the Current Account's Secondary Income component.

questions.question: What does a country's Financial Account balance specifically measure?
options: The difference between exports and imports of goods and services in a given year
The net acquisition of foreign financial assets minus the net incurrence of foreign liabilities
The total stock of foreign currency held by the central bank as official reserves
The sum of all interest and dividend payments received from foreign investments
questions.explanation: The Financial Account measures whether a country is a net lender (acquiring more foreign assets than liabilities) or a net borrower (incurring more liabilities than assets acquired).

questions.question: The one-year Yen interest rate is 0% and the one-year Dollar interest rate is 5%. The spot rate is 110 Yen per Dollar. What is the no-arbitrage one-year forward exchange rate?
options: 115.50 Yen per Dollar, reflecting the interest rate differential added to the spot rate
104.76 Yen per Dollar, calculated using the covered interest parity formula
110.00 Yen per Dollar, because forward rates always equal current spot rates
105.00 Yen per Dollar, the simple average of the spot rate and the interest differential
questions.explanation: Forward rate = $S \times (1 + r_{yen}) / (1 + r_{usd}) = 110 \times (1.00 / 1.05) \approx 104.76$ Yen per Dollar.

questions.question: What is exchange rate overshooting in response to a monetary policy change?
options: The central bank deliberately pushing the exchange rate beyond its fundamental value
The short-run exchange rate moving past its long-run equilibrium due to sticky goods prices
The nominal exchange rate changing while the real exchange rate remains perfectly constant
A speculative attack that causes the currency to collapse far below its fair value
questions.explanation: Because goods prices are sticky in the short run but financial markets adjust instantly, the exchange rate must temporarily move beyond its long-run equilibrium to clear the money market.

questions.question: What is sterilized intervention by a central bank in the foreign exchange market?
options: Allowing the currency to float completely freely without any central bank activity
Buying or selling foreign reserves while offsetting the money supply impact with domestic open-market operations
Permanently and irrevocably fixing the exchange rate at a specific target level
Banning all private foreign exchange transactions to prevent currency speculation
questions.explanation: Sterilized intervention aims to influence the exchange rate without affecting the domestic money supply or interest rates, by pairing FX intervention with offsetting bond sales or purchases.

questions.question: What does the Law of One Price predict about identical goods sold in different countries?
options: Prices will differ permanently based on local supply and demand conditions in each market
Identical goods should sell for the same price everywhere when expressed in a common currency
Each country may set its own price for traded goods through government regulation
The country with the highest production cost will always set the global market price
questions.explanation: LOOP assumes no transportation costs or trade barriers. Arbitrage should eliminate any persistent price differences for identical goods across different geographic markets.
